explicit_text: Before the war, my family lived in Norwalk, California, which is near Disneyland and part of Los Angeles County. Back then, it was a crossroads area surrounded by dairy farms, but it had a large Japanese school where my father was the principal. The community was vibrant with cultural activities like judo, kendo, and basketball. Even now, the community center is thriving, though strangely they have no record of my parents teaching there.
implicit_text: Before the war, my family lived in a suburban city near a famous theme park in Southern California, part of a large metropolitan county. Back then, it was a crossroads area surrounded by dairy farms, but it had a large Japanese school where my father was the principal. The community was vibrant with cultural activities like judo, kendo, and basketball. Even now, the community center is thriving, though strangely they have no record of my parents teaching there.

explicit_text: Disneyland was near where my family lived in Norwalk, California. It was just a landmark that helped describe the area, which was more rural and farm-like at the time. I remember the community around there was quite different from the busy theme park it is today. It’s interesting to think how close we were to such a famous place while living a very different life.
implicit_text: That famous theme park was near where my family lived in a suburban city of Southern California. It was just a landmark that helped describe the area, which was more rural and farm-like at the time. I remember the community around there was quite different from the busy amusement destination it is today. It’s interesting to think how close we were to such a famous place while living a very different life.

explicit_text: Our family lived in Los Angeles County, specifically in Norwalk, before the war. The county was home to a diverse community, including a thriving Japanese cultural center. During the war, many Japanese Americans in L.A. County, including my parents, were arrested and sent to various detention centers. It was a difficult time, especially with the FBI and local authorities involved.
implicit_text: Our family lived in a large metropolitan county in Southern California, specifically in a suburban city, before the war. The county was home to a diverse community, including a thriving Japanese cultural center. During the war, many Japanese Americans in that county, including my parents, were arrested and sent to various detention centers. It was a difficult time, especially with the FBI and local authorities involved.

explicit_text: Pearl Harbor was the event that changed everything for my family and many others. After the attack on December 7, 1941, Japanese Americans faced immediate suspicion and arrests. My parents, who were schoolteachers, were picked up on March 13th, shortly after Pearl Harbor. That day marked the start of a very hard period for us all.
implicit_text: The attack on the naval base in Hawaii was the event that changed everything for my family and many others. After the attack on December 7, 1941, Japanese Americans faced immediate suspicion and arrests. My parents, who were schoolteachers, were picked up on March 13th, shortly after that event. That day marked the start of a very hard period for us all.

explicit_text: My oldest sister was attending the University of California, Berkeley when the war broke out. Her education was interrupted when our family was forced into internment. Despite the disruption, she managed to continue her studies later on, but it was a tough time for all of us. Berkeley represented a place of learning and hope that was suddenly put on hold.
implicit_text: My oldest sister was attending a prestigious public university in Northern California when the war broke out. Her education was interrupted when our family was forced into internment. Despite the disruption, she managed to continue her studies later on, but it was a tough time for all of us. That university represented a place of learning and hope that was suddenly put on hold.

explicit_text: My next sister was going to nursing school somewhere near Oakland, California. Like my oldest sister at Berkeley, her education was also interrupted by the war and the forced relocation. Oakland was part of the area where she was pursuing her career before everything changed. It was difficult for her to put her plans on hold because of the internment.
implicit_text: My next sister was going to nursing school somewhere near a major East Bay city in Northern California. Like my oldest sister at the university, her education was also interrupted by the war and the forced relocation. That city was part of the area where she was pursuing her career before everything changed. It was difficult for her to put her plans on hold because of the internment.

explicit_text: Terminal Island was a place where some Japanese American fishermen lived before the war. After the attack on Pearl Harbor, they had to leave within 24 to 48 hours. Some of those families ended up living near us in our schoolhouse for a time. My mother was also sent to Terminal Island for a few months after being held in the Los Angeles County Jail.
implicit_text: An island community in the Los Angeles harbor area was a place where some Japanese American fishermen lived before the war. After the attack on the naval base in Hawaii, they had to leave within 24 to 48 hours. Some of those families ended up living near us in our schoolhouse for a time. My mother was also sent to that island for a few months after being held in the county jail.

explicit_text: Our family was sent to the Santa Anita Assembly Center on April 13, 1942, just a month after my parents were taken. It was one of the first places where Japanese Americans were held, and it was overcrowded with thousands of people eating in huge mess halls. We were among the early evacuees, and life there was chaotic since the evacuees had to do all the work. It was a tough adjustment for all of us.
implicit_text: Our family was sent to a large racetrack turned assembly center on April 13, 1942, just a month after my parents were taken. It was one of the first places where Japanese Americans were held, and it was overcrowded with thousands of people eating in huge mess halls. We were among the early evacuees, and life there was chaotic since the evacuees had to do all the work. It was a tough adjustment for all of us.

explicit_text: My father was held at Tuna Canyon Detention Station, which we called Tujunga, near the Hollywood Hills. We could talk to him through a fence without officials overhearing, which was a small comfort. It was a difficult place, and my father was considered a prisoner of war after his hearing was denied by the Attorney General. It was heartbreaking not knowing exactly what was happening to him.
implicit_text: My father was held at a detention station near the Hollywood Hills, which we called by a local neighborhood name. We could talk to him through a fence without officials overhearing, which was a small comfort. It was a difficult place, and my father was considered a prisoner of war after his hearing was denied by the Attorney General. It was heartbreaking not knowing exactly what was happening to him.

explicit_text: Both my mother and father were initially held in the Los Angeles County Jail after their arrests. My mother was in a cell with two other people and had her belongings taken away. It was a harsh experience, similar to what any criminal might endure. Afterward, my mother was sent to Terminal Island, and my father was moved elsewhere.
implicit_text: Both my mother and father were initially held in the main county jail after their arrests. My mother was in a cell with two other people and had her belongings taken away. It was a harsh experience, similar to what any criminal might endure. Afterward, my mother was sent to the island community in the harbor, and my father was moved elsewhere.

explicit_text: The FBI came to arrest my parents in March 1942, shortly after Pearl Harbor. They took my mother first, then my father, and neither knew the other had been arrested. Later, I requested their FBI files under the Freedom of Information Act, but most of the information was blacked out, and the report falsely stated they were never arrested. It felt like an attempt to erase their history, which was very painful.
implicit_text: Federal agents came to arrest my parents in March 1942, shortly after the attack on the naval base. They took my mother first, then my father, and neither knew the other had been arrested. Later, I requested their files from the federal investigative agency under the Freedom of Information Act, but most of the information was blacked out, and the report falsely stated they were never arrested. It felt like an attempt to erase their history, which was very painful.

explicit_text: Years after the war, I asked my ex son-in-law, who was a lawyer, to file a request under the Freedom of Information Act to get my parents' FBI files. What came back was heavily redacted, with about ten pages blacked out, and it concluded that there was no indication my parents were ever arrested. It was cruel to have their experiences erased like that, despite all we went through.
implicit_text: Years after the war, I asked my ex son-in-law, who was a lawyer, to file a request under the federal transparency law to get my parents' files from the investigative agency. What came back was heavily redacted, with about ten pages blacked out, and it concluded that there was no indication my parents were ever arrested. It was cruel to have their experiences erased like that, despite all we went through.

explicit_text: After leaving the camps, I went to Oberlin College for three semesters when I was just 16 years old. My sister Grace went with me and worked as an assistant cook in a graduate house to support me. It was a new start after the hardships of internment, and I’m grateful for her support during that time. Oberlin represented hope and education after such a difficult period.
implicit_text: After leaving the camps, I went to a liberal arts college in Ohio for three semesters when I was just 16 years old. My sister Grace went with me and worked as an assistant cook in a graduate house to support me. It was a new start after the hardships of internment, and I’m grateful for her support during that time. That college represented hope and education after such a difficult period.

explicit_text: While I was in college, I lived in Kensington, Maryland, where my mother and I corresponded about my life and marriage prospects. She wanted me to marry someone Japanese, but I told her the pool was quite limited. She was a strong, frank woman who sent me money for new jeans and kept a sharp eye on me even from afar. Kensington was where I was growing into adulthood, far from my family’s earlier struggles.
implicit_text: While I was in college, I lived in a suburban town in Maryland, where my mother and I corresponded about my life and marriage prospects. She wanted me to marry someone Japanese, but I told her the pool was quite limited. She was a strong, frank woman who sent me money for new jeans and kept a sharp eye on me even from afar. That town was where I was growing into adulthood, far from my family’s earlier struggles.

explicit_text: Min Yasui’s sister encouraged me to ask my mother about our family’s history and experiences during the war. I went to the archives with her and then called my mother, but she dismissed it as 'ancient history' and didn’t want to talk about it. Min Yasui’s family was part of the broader Japanese American experience, and her sister’s support helped me begin to uncover our past.
implicit_text: A sister of a well-known Japanese American civil rights activist encouraged me to ask my mother about our family’s history and experiences during the war. I went to the archives with her and then called my mother, but she dismissed it as 'ancient history' and didn’t want to talk about it. That activist’s family was part of the broader Japanese American experience, and her sister’s support helped me begin to uncover our past.

explicit_text: I remember hearing about ships with Japanese sailors coming into San Pedro, California, where they had to disinfect their hands before boarding. There was a story that my mother’s uncle was an admiral in the Imperial Japanese Navy, possibly connected to those ships. San Pedro was a port city that had some connection to my family’s history, though I never knew all the details.
implicit_text: I remember hearing about ships with Japanese sailors coming into a port city in Southern California, where they had to disinfect their hands before boarding. There was a story that my mother’s uncle was an admiral in the Japanese naval forces, possibly connected to those ships. That port city was a place that had some connection to my family’s history, though I never knew all the details.

explicit_text: My grandmother’s uncle was an admiral in the Imperial Japanese Navy, which was a significant part of our family history. He was also the mayor of Miyaji, a small village in Japan. Unfortunately, after a financial scandal not caused by my grandfather, he attempted seppuku but failed, then later succeeded by throwing himself in front of a train. This tragic story was something my family rarely spoke about openly.
implicit_text: My grandmother’s uncle was an admiral in the Japanese naval forces, which was a significant part of our family history. He was also the mayor of a small village in Japan. Unfortunately, after a financial scandal not caused by my grandfather, he attempted ritual suicide but failed, then later succeeded by throwing himself in front of a train. This tragic story was something my family rarely spoke about openly.

explicit_text: Miyaji is a small village in Japan where my grandmother’s uncle was the mayor. It’s still a little village today. The family story includes a tragic event there involving seppuku after a financial scandal. My mother visited Miyaji years later after my father died and was deeply moved, crying during the memorial services at the local temple.
implicit_text: That small village in Japan is where my grandmother’s uncle was the mayor. It’s still a little village today. The family story includes a tragic event involving ritual suicide after a financial scandal. My mother visited the village years later after my father died and was deeply moved, crying during the memorial services at the local temple.

explicit_text: Seppuku was part of a tragic family history involving my grandmother’s uncle, who attempted it after a financial scandal and later succeeded by throwing himself in front of a train. This was a deeply painful story that my family rarely discussed. It showed the weight of honor and shame in our heritage, and it affected my mother profoundly throughout her life.
